content.text: Adding a question mark after a property name makes it optional — the property can be absent from an object, or explicitly set to undefined. TypeScript widens the property type to include undefined automatically.

attrs.alt: Optional vs Readonly members comparison diagram
attrs.title: Optional vs Readonly

content.text: An optional function parameter and an optional interface property are similar in appearance but governed by different rules. Optional parameters must come after required ones; optional properties have no ordering constraint.

The readonly modifier prevents a property from being reassigned after its initial value is set. It is enforced only at compile time — there is no runtime check. The property is still writable during object construction.
. At runtime the property is a normal writable property. Use
if you need a runtime guarantee.
Both modifiers can appear together on the same property. The order of modifiers does not matter.
Utility Types for Optional and Readonly
TypeScript ships four utility types that systematically add or remove optional and readonly modifiers across every property of a type.
Angular Patterns with Optional and Readonly

Defining an interface for component inputs gives you autocomplete, type safety, and a single source of truth for what the component accepts. Optional properties map to inputs that have defaults; readonly signals enforce that the signal reference itself does not change.

The built-in Readonly<T> is shallow — it only marks the top-level properties as readonly. Nested objects can still be mutated. A recursive mapped type provides a deep readonly guarantee at the type level.
Optional vs Nullable — A Common Pitfall
Optional (?) and nullable (| null) are different concepts that are often confused. Understanding the distinction prevents subtle bugs, especially when working with JSON APIs and Angular forms.
The readonly modifier extends to array types and tuples. A readonly array cannot have elements pushed, popped, or spliced. Readonly tuples also prevent element reassignment.
When a class implements an interface with readonly members, the class must also mark those properties readonly. TypeScript checks this at the point of assignment, not just at the interface level.
Excess Property Checks and Optional Members

Optional members interact with excess property checks in a subtle way. TypeScript allows extra properties when assigning a variable to an interface, but the object literal check catches typos in optional property names.

Optional members (?) and readonly members form the two most important property-level modifiers in TypeScript interfaces. Optional properties allow objects to omit non-essential fields; readonly properties prevent accidental mutation after object creation.
; object can omit the property entirely.
: compile-time guard only — prevents reassignment, not deep mutation.
) to create modified copies of readonly objects.
In Angular, optional inputs map to signal inputs with
; readonly state belongs in signals updated via
(the former is type-level, the latter is runtime).
